items.q: Was the Big Bang an explosion of matter into pre-existing space?
items.correct: No — it was the expansion of space itself, beginning from a hot dense state; there was no 'outside' for matter to expand into.
items.misconception: Yes — it was a giant explosion at a point, and galaxies are still flying outwards from it.
items.random_wrong: Yes — it was a bomb-like event that destroyed an earlier universe.
items.why: Pop-science imagery of a fiery explosion encourages the wrong mental model; in fact every point in the universe was once 'the centre'.

items.q: What does the cosmological redshift of distant galaxies indicate?
items.correct: Space itself is expanding, stretching the wavelength of light from distant galaxies as it travels.
items.misconception: Galaxies are moving through space away from us, like objects flying outward from a centre.
items.random_wrong: The galaxies are turning red because they are getting older.
items.why: Students apply the Doppler effect literally; the expansion of space is a more subtle picture they often miss.

items.q: Does Hooke's law apply to a spring for any amount of stretching?
items.correct: No — it only holds up to the limit of proportionality; beyond that the relationship between force and extension is no longer linear.
items.misconception: Yes — Hooke's law applies to any spring at any extension.
items.random_wrong: Only when the force is small enough to be ignored.
items.why: Students apply F = kx universally without noting the limit of proportionality, leading to over-prediction at large extensions.
Correct answer: 2.7 K. “0 K” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Big Bang nucleosynthesis predicts He fraction (by mass) ≈
items.why: Correct answer: 25%. “1%” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: λ_max of CMB (mm)? T = 2.7 K, Wien const 2.9e-3
items.why: The real figure is 1.1. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
